name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ فَكَذَّبُوا۟ عَبْدَنَا وَقَالُوا۟ مَجْنُونٌ وَٱزْدُجِرَ (٩)
```

```english
The people of Noah impugned before them. So they impugned Our servant and said, ‘A crazy man,’ and he was reviled. (9)
```

```arabic
فَدَعَا رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَغْلُوبٌ فَٱنتَصِرْ (١٠)
```

```english
Thereat he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘I have been overcome, so help [me].’ (10)
```

```arabic
فَفَتَحْنَآ أَبْوَٰبَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَآءٍ مُّنْهَمِرٍ (١١)
```

```english
Then We opened the gates of the sky with pouring waters, (11)
```

```arabic
وَفَجَّرْنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَٱلْتَقَى ٱلْمَآءُ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ (١٢)
```

```english
and We made the earth burst forth with springs, and the waters met for a preordained purpose. (12)
```

```arabic
وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْوَٰحٍ وَدُسُرٍ (١٣)
```

```english
We bore him on a vessel made of planks and nails, (13)
```

```arabic
تَجْرِى بِأَعْيُنِنَا جَزَآءً لِّمَن كَانَ كُفِرَ (١٤)
```

```english
which sailed [over the flood waters] in Our sight, as a retribution for him who was met with disbelief. (14)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (١٥)
```

```english
Certainly We have left it as a sign; so is there anyone who will be admonished? (15)
```

```arabic
فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (١٦)
```

```english
So how were My punishment and warnings? (16)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (١٧)
```

```english
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (17)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (١٨)
```

```english
[The people of] ‘Ad impugned [their apostle]. So how were My punishment and warnings? (18)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِى يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُّسْتَمِرٍّ (١٩)
```

```english
Indeed We unleashed upon them an icy gale on an incessantly ill-fated day, (19)
```

```arabic
تَنزِعُ ٱلنَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجَازُ نَخْلٍ مُّنقَعِرٍ (٢٠)
```

```english
knocking down people as if they were trunks of uprooted palm trees. (20)
```

```arabic
فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (٢١)
```

```english
So how were My punishment and warnings?! (21)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (٢٢)
```

```english
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (22)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِٱلنُّذُرِ (٢٣)
```

```english
[The people of] Thamud denied the warnings, (23)
```

```arabic
فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرًا مِّنَّا وَٰحِدًا نَّتَّبِعُهُۥٓ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ وَسُعُرٍ (٢٤)
```

```english
and they said, ‘Are we to follow a lone human from ourselves?! Indeed then we would be in error and madness.’ (24)
```

```arabic
أَءُلْقِىَ ٱلذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ (٢٥)
```

```english
‘Has the Reminder been cast upon him from among us? No, he is a self-conceited liar.’ (25)
```

```arabic
سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَّنِ ٱلْكَذَّابُ ٱلْأَشِرُ (٢٦)
```

```english
‘Tomorrow they will know who is a self-conceited liar. (26)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا مُرْسِلُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَّهُمْ فَٱرْتَقِبْهُمْ وَٱصْطَبِرْ (٢٧)
```

```english
We are sending the She-camel as a trial for them; so watch them and be steadfast. (27)
```

```arabic
وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ ٱلْمَآءَ قِسْمَةٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُّحْتَضَرٌ (٢٨)
```

```english
Inform them that the water is to be shared between them; each of them showing up at his turn.’ (28)
```

```arabic
فَنَادَوْا۟ صَاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعَاطَىٰ فَعَقَرَ (٢٩)
```

```english
But they called their companion, and he took [a knife] and hamstrung [her]. (29)
```

```arabic
فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (٣٠)
```

```english
So how were My punishment and My warnings?! (30)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً فَكَانُوا۟ كَهَشِيمِ ٱلْمُحْتَظِرِ (٣١)
```

```english
We sent against them a single Cry, and they became like the dry sticks of a corral builder. (31)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (٣٢)
```

```english
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (32)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍۭ بِٱلنُّذُرِ (٣٣)
```

```english
And the people of Lot denied the warnings. (33)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا إِلَّآ ءَالَ لُوطٍ ۖ نَّجَّيْنَـٰهُم بِسَحَرٍ (٣٤)
```

```english
We unleashed upon them a rain of stones, excepting the family of Lot, whom We delivered at dawn (34)
```

```arabic
نِّعْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى مَن شَكَرَ (٣٥)
```

```english
as a blessing from Us. Thus do We reward those who give thanks. (35)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَنذَرَهُم بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا۟ بِٱلنُّذُرِ (٣٦)
```

```english
He had already warned them of Our punishment, but they disputed the warnings. (36)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ رَٰوَدُوهُ عَن ضَيْفِهِۦ فَطَمَسْنَآ أَعْيُنَهُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (٣٧)
```

```english
They even solicited of him his guests, whereat We blotted out their eyes, [saying,] ‘Taste My punishment and My warnings!’ (37)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ صَبَّحَهُم بُكْرَةً عَذَابٌ مُّسْتَقِرٌّ (٣٨)
```

```english
Early at dawn there visited them an abiding punishment: (38)
```

```arabic
فَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِى وَنُذُرِ (٣٩)
```

```english
‘Taste My punishment and warnings!’ (39)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِن مُّدَّكِرٍ (٤٠)
```

```english
Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonishment. So is there anyone who will be admonished? (40)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ جَآءَ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ ٱلنُّذُرُ (٤١)
```

```english
Certainly the warnings did come to Pharaoh’s clan (41)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا كُلِّهَا فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُّقْتَدِرٍ (٤٢)
```

```english
who denied all of Our signs. So We seized them with the seizing of One [who is] all-mighty, Omnipotent. (42)
```

## (Commentary)

This is an allusion to some of the reports concerning bygone nations that contain a deterrent (مزدجر). Among them, the author specifically mentions the people of Nuh, ‘Ad, Thamud, the people of Lut, and the clan of Pharaoh. He recounts their reports and repeats for the current audience a summary of the stories previously narrated to them, and the painful torment and terrifying punishment that resulted from their denial of Allah’s signs and His messengers, as a confirmation of His saying: **وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْبَاءِ مَا فِيهِ مُزْدَجَرٌ** — "Certainly there has come to them reports containing a deterrent" (54:4).

To reinforce this confirmation and to illustrate the heart-striking deterrence contained in these cautionary tales, He followed each story by addressing them with His saying: **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ** — "So how were My punishment and My warnings?" (54:16). He then followed this by mentioning the purpose of the warning and intimidation, saying: **وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ** — "Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonition. So is there anyone who will be admonished?" (54:17).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ فَكَذَّبُوا عَبْدَنَا وَقَالُوا مَجْنُونٌ وَازْدُجِرَ** — "The people of Noah denied before them; so they denied Our servant and said, ‘A crazy man,’ and he was reviled" (54:9). The first mention of denial (التكذيب) is treated as an intransitive verb, meaning "they practiced denial." His saying: **فَكَذَّبُوا عَبْدَنَا** — "so they denied Our servant" etc., is an explanation of it, similar to His saying: **وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ رَبَّهُ فَقَالَ** — "And Noah called out to his Lord and said..." (11:45).

It has been said that the first "denial" refers to absolute denial—that is, their denial of the messengers—while the second refers specifically to the denial of Nuh. This is similar to His saying in Surah al-Shu‘ara: **كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** — "The people of Noah denied the apostles" (26:105). Thus, the meaning would be: the people of Nuh denied the messengers, and consequently, they denied Nuh. This is a plausible interpretation.

It has also been said that the branching of one "denial" from another indicates a succession of denials over the long duration of his mission; whenever one denying generation passed away, another denying generation succeeded them. However, this is a far-fetched meaning.

Similar to this is the view of some who say the first "denial" refers to their intention and the second to their action.

In His saying: **فَكَذَّبُوا عَبْدَنَا** — "so they denied Our servant," the reference to Nuh (peace be upon him) as "Our servant" (عبدنا) in such a context is an exaltation of his station and a glorification of his affair. It also indicates that their denial of him returns to Allah the Exalted, for he is a servant who possesses nothing, and whatever he has belongs to Allah.

Regarding His saying: **وَقَالُوا مَجْنُونٌ وَازْدُجِرَ** — "and they said, ‘A crazy man,’ and he was reviled," the meaning of "reviled" (الازدجار) here refers to the jinn driving him away as a result of madness. The meaning is: they did not stop at mere denial but attributed madness to him, saying he is possessed and that the jinn have driven him to madness, such that he only speaks out of their agitation, and his speech has nothing to do with heavenly revelation (الوحي السماوي).

It has also been said that the omitted subject of "reviled" is the people themselves. The meaning would then be: the people deterred him (ازدجره) from his mission and preaching through various forms of harm and intimidation. Perhaps the first meaning is more apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَدَعَا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ** — "So he invoked his Lord, ‘I am overcome, so help [me]!’" (54:10). Help (الانتصار) here means taking vengeance. His saying: **أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ** — "I am overcome" means by force and coercion rather than by argument. This supplication is a summary of his detailed prayer, which is mentioned in Surah Nuh, while the details of his arguments are found in Surah Hud and elsewhere.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَفَتَحْنَا أَبْوَابَ السَّمَاءِ بِمَاءٍ مُنْهَمِرٍ** — "Then We opened the gates of the sky with pouring water" (54:11). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Pouring (الهمر) is the heavy shedding of tears or water, and 'in-himar' (الانهمار) is the act of pouring down." The opening of the gates of the sky—which is the atmosphere—with pouring water is a representative metaphor (استعارة تمثيلية) for the intensity of the water's descent and the continuous flow of rain, as if it were stored behind a locked gate preventing its fall, which was then opened, causing it to pour down with the utmost force.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَفَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَالْتَقَى الْمَاءُ عَلَىٰ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ** — "and We made the earth gush forth with springs, so the waters met for a matter already ordained" (54:12). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Gushing (التفجير) is the splitting of the earth for water, and springs (العيون) is the plural of 'spring' (عين الماء), which is what wells up from the earth in a circular fashion, like the circularity of an animal's eye."

The meaning is: We made the earth into springs gushing with water, flowing in a harmonious and continuous manner.

His saying: **فَالْتَقَى الْمَاءُ عَلَىٰ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ** — "so the waters met for a matter already ordained" means the two waters—the water of the sky and the water of the earth—met, settling upon a matter decreed by Allah the Exalted; that is, according to what He had measured, without deficiency or excess, and without haste or delay.

"The water" (الماء) is a generic noun referring to both the water of the sky and the water of the earth, which is why it was not used in the dual form. The "matter already ordained" refers to the description Allah decreed for this Deluge.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَحَمَلْنَاهُ عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْوَاحٍ وَدُسُرٍ** — "And We carried him on a vessel of planks and nails" (54:13). What is meant by "that of planks and nails" is the ark. Planks (الألواح) is the plural of 'plank' (لوح), which is the wood laid upon one another in a ship. Nails (الدسر) is the plural of 'disar' (دسار) or 'dusur', which is the nail used to fasten the planks in a ship. Other meanings have been suggested, but they do not suit the verse as well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَجْرِي بِأَعْيُنِنَا جَزَاءً لِمَنْ كَانَ كُفِرَ** — "sailing before Our eyes, as a reward for him who had been rejected" (54:14). That is, the ark sailed upon the water encompassing the earth under various forms of Our supervision, protection, and guardianship. It has also been said to mean it sailed before the eyes of Our friends (أولياء) and the angels We entrusted with it.

His saying: **جَزَاءً لِمَنْ كَانَ كُفِرَ** — "as a reward for him who had been rejected" means the sailing of the ark in this manner, including the salvation of those within it from destruction, was a reward for the one who had been disbelieved in—namely Nuh (peace be upon him), whose people rejected him and his mission. Thus, the verse carries the same meaning as His saying: **وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ ... إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "And We delivered him and his family from the great agony... Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous" (37:76-80).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ تَرَكْنَاهَا آيَةً فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ** — "Certainly We have left it as a sign. So is there anyone who will be admonished?" (54:15). The pronoun in "We have left it" (تركناها) refers to the ark, as indicated by the context, and the 'lam' is for an oath. The meaning is: I swear, We have preserved that ark by which We saved Nuh and those with him, and We made it a sign (آية) for whoever would take heed. So is there any mindful person who will be reminded by it of His oneness, the Exalted, and that the call of His prophets is truth, and that His seizing is painful and severe? This meaning necessitates the ark's preservation until the time these verses were revealed as a sign indicating the event of the Deluge and serving as a reminder of it. Some have said in the commentary of this verse, as reported: "Allah preserved Nuh's ark on Mount Judi until the early members of this Ummah saw it." We mentioned in the commentary of Surah Hud, at the end of the discussions regarding the story of Nuh, a report that pieces of wood from a disintegrated ship were found on one of the peaks of Mount Ararat, which is Judi; so refer back to it.

It has also been said that the pronoun in "We have left it" refers to the aforementioned story, insofar as it is His act.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ** — "So how were My punishment and My warnings?" (54:16). Warnings (النذر) is the plural of 'nadhir' (نذير) in the sense of warning (الإنذار). It has also been said to be a verbal noun meaning warning. It appears that "was" (كان) is an incomplete verb (ناقصة), its subject being "My punishment" (عذابي) and its predicate being "how" (فكيف). It is also possible that it is a complete verb (تامة), its subject being "My punishment," and "how" being a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for it.

In any case, the interrogation is for the purpose of intimidation (التهويل), recording the severity of the punishment and the truth of the warning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ** — "Certainly We have made the Quran simple for the sake of admonition. So is there anyone who will be admonished?" (54:17). Simplification (التيسير) means making easy. Making the Quran simple for admonition means presenting it in a way that makes its objectives easy to understand for both the commoner and the elite, for simple minds and deep thinkers, each according to their capacity of understanding.

It could also mean the descent of its high realities and lofty objectives from the horizon of ordinary understanding to the stage of Arabic speech, so that general intellects can grasp it, as inferred from His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** — "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother Book with Us, sublime and wise" (43:3-4).

Admonition (الذكر) refers to remembering Him, the Exalted, through His names, attributes, or acts. It is stated in *al-Mufradat*: "Admonition (الذكر) is sometimes used to refer to a state of the soul by which a person can preserve the knowledge they acquire—it is like 'preservation' (الحفظ), except that preservation is used in consideration of its storage, while 'dhikr' is used in consideration of its presence. Sometimes it is used for the presence of a thing in the heart or in speech. Thus it is said: 'dhikr' is of two types: 'dhikr' of the heart and 'dhikr' of the tongue. Each of these is of two kinds: 'dhikr' after forgetfulness and 'dhikr' not after forgetfulness but from continuous preservation. Every speech is also called 'dhikr'."

The meaning of the verse is: I swear, We have made the Quran easy so that one may be reminded by it, thereby remembering Allah the Exalted and His affairs. So is there any mindful person who will be reminded by it, thus believing in Allah and submitting to the true religion He calls toward?

The verse is a general call to mindfulness through the Quran after recording the truth of the warning and the severity of the punishment warned against.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ** — "‘Ad denied. So how were My punishment and My warnings?" (54:18). This is the start of another story among those containing a deterrent. It is not coordinated with what preceded it—nor are the subsequent stories—because each of these stories is independent and sufficient for deterrence (الزجر), restraint (الردع), and exhortation (العظة), if they were to take heed.

His saying: **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ** is intended to direct the hearts of the listeners to what is about to be conveyed to them regarding the nature of the terrifying punishment in His saying: **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا** — "Indeed We sent..." etc. It is not intended for intimidation and recording the severity of the punishment and the truth of the warning like the previous one; otherwise, His saying: **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ** etc. would have been repeated afterward. This has been suggested, and it is a plausible view.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِي يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُسْتَمِرٍّ** — "Indeed We sent against them a freezing wind on a day of unrelenting ill omen" (54:19). This is an explanation of what was asked about in His saying: "So how were My punishment and My warnings?" A freezing wind (الصرصر)—according to *al-Majma‘*—is a wind with a violent blast. Ill omen (النحس) is a verbal noun like 'nuhusah' (النحوسة), meaning misfortune (الشؤم). Unrelenting (مستمر) is an adjective for "ill omen." The meaning of sending the wind on a day of unrelenting ill omen is sending it on a day characterized by misfortune and bad luck relative to them, which continued against them such that no good or salvation could be hoped for.

"Day" (اليوم) here refers to a period of time, not the day that equals one-seventh of a week, because He says in another place: **فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِي أَيَّامٍ نَحِسَاتٍ** — "So We sent against them a freezing wind on ill-fated days" (41:16), and in another place: **سَخَّرَهَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَبْعَ لَيَالٍ وَثَمَانِيَةَ أَيَّامٍ حُسُومًا** — "which He let loose on them for seven nights and eight days consecutively" (69:7).

Some have interpreted "ill omen" (النحس) as cold.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَنْزِعُ النَّاسَ كَأَنَّهُمْ أَعْجازُ نَخْلٍ مُنْقَعِرٍ** — "uprooting the people as if they were trunks of uprooted palm trees" (54:20). The subject of "uprooting" (تنزع) is the pronoun referring to the wind; that is, the wind uproots the people from the earth. Trunks (أعجاز) of palm trees refers to their lower parts. Uprooted (المنقعر) means pulled out from its root. The meaning is clear, and the verse alludes to the large physical stature of the people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي** — to His saying — **مُدَّكِرٍ** (54:21-22). The commentary on these two verses has already been provided.

## ( A Discourse on the Auspiciousness and Inauspiciousness of Days, Omens, and Augury in Several Sections )

**1 - On the Auspiciousness and Inauspiciousness of Days:** The inauspiciousness (نحوسة) of a day or any period of time means that the events occurring within it result in nothing but evil, and that actions—or a specific type of action—within it are not blessed for the doer. Auspiciousness (سعادة) is its opposite.

We have no way to establish a proof for the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of any particular day or time. The quantitative nature of time consists of similar parts and portions. Furthermore, we do not encompass the active causes and reasons that influence the occurrence of events and the existence of actions, such that it might become clear to us that the cycle of a day or a piece of time arises from causes and reasons necessitating its auspiciousness or inauspiciousness. For this reason, sufficient empirical experience is not possible, as it would depend on isolating the subject for its effect so that it could be known that the effect is indeed its own—and this is unknown in this context.

For the same reason, we have no way to establish a proof for the negation of auspiciousness and inauspiciousness, just as there is no way for their affirmation. Even if their reality seems remote, remoteness is not the same as impossibility. This is from the perspective of rational inquiry.

As for the perspective of religious law (النظر الشرعي), the Book mentions inauspiciousness and its opposite. The Exalted says: **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِي يَوْمِ نَحْسٍ مُسْتَمِرٍّ** — "Indeed We sent against them a freezing wind on a day of unrelenting ill omen" (54:19), and He says: **فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِيحًا صَرْصَرًا فِي أَيَّامٍ نَحِسَاتٍ** — "So We sent against them a freezing wind on ill-fated days" (41:16). However, it does not appear from the context of the story or the indication of the two verses anything more than that the inauspiciousness and misfortune were specific to the very time in which the wind blew upon them as a punishment—which was seven nights and eight consecutive days during which the punishment continued against them—without it cycling with the weeks, which is obvious (otherwise all time would be inauspicious), nor with the cycles of months and years.

The Exalted also says: **وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبَارَكَةٍ** — "By the Manifest Book! Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night" (44:2-3). What is meant is the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), which Allah the Exalted describes by saying: **لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ** — "The Night of Ordainment is better than a thousand months" (97:3). It is clear that the blessing and auspiciousness of this night are due to its association with great matters of divine internal emanations (الإفاضات الباطنية الإلهية) and spiritual acts, such as the firming of the decree, the descent of the angels and the Spirit, and its being a peace. The Exalted says: **فِيهَا يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ** — "In it is distinct every wise command" (44:4), and He says: **تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ سَلَامٌ هِيَ حَتَّىٰ مَطْلَعِ الْفَجْرِ** — "In it the angels and the Spirit descend, by the leave of their Lord, with every command. It is peace until the rising of the dawn" (97:4-5).

The meaning of its blessing and auspiciousness returns to the merit of worship and ritual acts within it, the abundance of its reward, and the proximity of divine care (العناية الإلهية) within it for those who turn toward the court of Might and Grandeur.

As for the Sunnah, there are very many narrations regarding the auspicious and inauspicious days of the week, the days of the Arabic months, the days of the Persian months, and the days of the Roman months. These narrations are extremely numerous and are preserved in the collections of hadith; most of them are weak, being 'mursal' or 'marfu‘' narrations, although some are not devoid of reliability in terms of their chains of transmission.

Regarding the narrations that count certain days as inauspicious—such as Wednesday, and the "Wednesday that does not recur" (the last Wednesday of the month), seven days of every Arabic month, two days of every Roman month, and the like—many of them, especially those dealing with the inauspiciousness of the days of the week and the Arabic months, justify the day's inauspiciousness by the occurrence of bitter, undesirable events according to religious taste, such as the passing of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the martyrdom of al-Husayn (عليه السلام), the casting of Ibrahim (عليه السلام) into the fire, the descent of punishment upon such-and-such a nation, the creation of the Fire, and other such events.

It is understood that counting these days as inauspicious and ill-omened, and avoiding the pursuit of desired matters and the seeking of needs that a person finds pleasure in attaining during them, serves to establish godwariness (التقوى) and strengthen the religious spirit. Conversely, not paying attention to them and persisting in the pursuit of whatever the soul desires at any time constitutes a turning away from the Truth, a violation of the sanctity of religion, and a disparagement of its friends. Thus, the inauspiciousness of these days returns to aspects of spiritual wretchedness (الشقاء المعنوي) arising from conventional causes and reasons linked in some way to these days, which produce a kind of religious wretchedness for one who pays no heed to them.

Furthermore, it has been reported in several of these narrations that one should seek refuge in Allah through charity, fasting, prayer, or the recitation of some of the Quran, etc., to repel the inauspiciousness of these days. For instance, in the *Majalis* of Ibn al-Shaykh, with his chain to Sahl ibn Ya‘qub, nicknamed Abu Nuwas, from Imam al-‘Askari (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "I said: 'My master, in most of these days there are obstacles to one's goals—due to the inauspiciousness and fears mentioned regarding them—so can you guide me to protection from the fears within them, for necessity calls me to attend to my needs during them?' He said to me: 'O Sahl, for our Shi‘a, our guardianship (الولاية) is a protection—if they were to traverse with it the depths of the overwhelming seas and the vast, trackless deserts (سباسب) among predators, wolves, and enemies from the jinn and humans, they would be safe from their fears through their guardianship of us. So trust in Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, be sincere in your devotion to your pure Imams, and head wherever you wish and pursue whatever you intend.'" The hadith continues.

Then he (عليه السلام) commanded him to recite something from the Quran and a supplication to repel the inauspiciousness and misfortune and to pursue whatever he intended.

In *al-Khisal*, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Riyāḥ al-Fallāḥ: "I saw Abu Ibrahim (عليه السلام) undergoing cupping on a Friday. I said: 'May I be your ransom, you undergo cupping on a Friday?' He said: 'I recite Ayat al-Kursi. Whenever your blood becomes agitated, whether by night or by day, recite Ayat al-Kursi and undergo cupping.'"

Also in *al-Khisal*, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Daqqaq: "I wrote to Abu al-Hasan the Second (عليه السلام) asking him about traveling on the Wednesday that does not recur. He (عليه السلام) wrote: 'Whoever travels on the Wednesday that does not recur, in opposition to the people of augury (أهل الطيرة), shall be protected from every blight, cured of every malady, and Allah will fulfill his need.' He wrote to him another time asking about cupping on the Wednesday that does not recur, and he (عليه السلام) wrote: 'Whoever undergoes cupping on the Wednesday that does not recur, in opposition to the people of augury, shall be cured of every blight, protected from every malady, and his cupping marks shall not turn green.'"

In the same vein is what is in *Tuhaf al-‘Uqul*: al-Husayn ibn Mas‘ud said: "I entered upon Abu al-Hasan ‘Ali ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام) after I had injured my finger, a rider had collided with me and bruised my shoulder, and I had entered a crowd where they tore some of my clothes. I said: 'May Allah protect me from your evil, O day! How ill-omened you are!' He (عليه السلام) said to me: 'O Hasan, do you say this while you frequent us, casting your blame upon one who is blameless?'"

al-Hasan said: "My reason returned to me and I realized my error, so I said: 'My master, I seek Allah's forgiveness.' He said: 'O Hasan, what is the fault of the days, such that you have come to see them as ill-omened when you are recompensed for your deeds within them?' al-Hasan said: 'I shall seek Allah's forgiveness forever; this is my repentance, O son of the Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'It does not benefit you, but Allah punishes you for blaming them for that which they are not to be blamed. Do you not know, O Hasan, that Allah is the One who rewards, punishes, and recompenses for deeds, both sooner and later?' I said: 'Yes, my master.' He said: 'Do not repeat this, and do not attribute to the days any role in the judgment of Allah.' al-Hasan said: 'Yes, my master.'"

From the previous narrations—and there are others like them in meaning—it is inferred that the criterion for the inauspiciousness of these ill-fated days is the augury (تطير) of the common people regarding them. Augury has a psychological effect, as will be discussed. These narrations treat the inauspiciousness that comes from the direction of augury by turning the soul away from augury if the person is strong enough for that, and by seeking refuge in Allah the Glorified and clinging to Him through a Quranic recitation or a supplication if they are not personally strong enough.

Some have interpreted these narrations that concede the inauspiciousness of certain days as being based on dissimulation (التقية), and this is not far-fetched. Pessimism and optimism regarding times, places, conditions, and states are characteristics of the common folk; much of this exists among them across various nations and groups, despite their diversity and separation, from ancient times until our day. Even among the elite in the early period of Islam, there were circulating narrations on this matter which they attributed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and which no one could easily reject. As in the book *al-Musalsalat*, with its chain to al-Fadl ibn al-Rabi‘: "I was one day with my master al-Ma’mun, and we wanted to set out on a Wednesday. al-Ma’mun said: 'It is a disliked day. I heard my father al-Rashid say: I heard al-Mahdi say: I heard al-Mansur say: I heard my father Muhammad ibn ‘Ali say: I heard my father ‘Ali say: I heard my father ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas say: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) say: The last Wednesday of the month is a day of unrelenting ill omen.'"

As for the narrations indicating auspicious days of the week and others, the explanation for them is similar to what was previously alluded to in the reports indicating inauspiciousness from the first perspective. These reports justify the blessing of the days they count as auspicious by the occurrence of great blessed events in the view of religion, such as the birth of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his mission. As it is reported: "He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) supplicated, saying: 'O Allah, bless my Ummah in its early hours on its Saturdays and Thursdays.'" And it is reported: "Allah made iron soft for Dawud (عليه السلام) on a Tuesday," and that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to set out for travel on Fridays, and that "the One" (الأحد) is among the names of Allah the Exalted.

Thus, it has become clear from the preceding—despite its length—that the reports concerning the auspiciousness and inauspiciousness of days do not indicate anything more than their basis in events linked to religion that necessitate beauty or ugliness according to religious taste or according to the influence of souls. As for the characterization of a day or any piece of time with the quality of being fortunate or unfortunate, or its being distinguished by ontological properties (خواص تكوينية) arising from natural ontological causes and reasons, this is not the case. Any reports that appear to contradict this are either to be interpreted as dissimulation or are not to be relied upon.

**2 - On the Auspiciousness and Inauspiciousness of the Stars** and the influence of celestial configurations on earthly events in terms of auspiciousness and inauspiciousness. The discussion of this from the perspective of rational inquiry is like the discussion of the auspiciousness and inauspiciousness of days; there is no way to establish a proof for any of it, such as the auspiciousness of the Sun and Jupiter, the conjunction of the two auspicious planets, the inauspiciousness of Mars, the conjunction of the two inauspicious planets, or the Moon being in Scorpio.

Yes, the ancient Indian astrologers believed that earthly events were linked to celestial configurations absolutely, whether the configurations of fixed stars or planets. Others saw this link between events and the configurations of the seven planets specifically, excluding the fixed stars. They assigned various properties and effects to their different configurations, called "astrological judgments" (أحكام النجوم), believing that whenever a configuration is realized, its effects follow.

These people are divided: some say the planetary bodies are living, sentient, and volitional beings that perform their acts through efficient causality (العلية الفاعلية); others say they are bodies without souls that exert their effect through efficient causality, or they are preparatory factors (معدات) for the act of the Exalted, who is the true Agent of events; or that the stars and their configurations are signs (علامات) for events without being agents or preparatory factors; or that none of these links exist between them and events even as signs, but rather it is the custom of Allah to bring about such-and-such an event during such-and-such a celestial configuration.

None of these judgments are permanent or consistent such that a specific judgment necessarily follows a specific configuration; sometimes they prove true and sometimes false. However, the wondrous stories and accounts that have reached us regarding their deductions suggest that there is some link between celestial configurations and earthly events, though it is partial (في الجملة) rather than universal (بالجملة), just as some narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) confirm this in a similar way.

Accordingly, it is not possible to judge definitively that a certain star or configuration is auspicious or inauspicious. As for the fundamental link between celestial and earthly events and configurations, it is not within the power of a critical researcher to deny that.

As for the claim that the stars or celestial configurations possess influence over what is below them—whether they are said to possess rational souls or not—this does not contradict any of the necessities of religion, unless it is claimed that they are creators and originators of what is below them without this terminating in the Exalted, which would be polytheism (شرك). However, no one claims this, not even the Sabian idolaters who worship the stars. Or if it is claimed that they are the governors of the cosmic order, independent in their governance, which would be a claim of Lordship (ربوبية) necessitating worship, and thus polytheism, as is the case with the Sabian star-worshippers.

As for the narrations regarding the influence of the stars in terms of auspiciousness, inauspiciousness, confirmation, and denial, they are very numerous and fall into several categories:

Among them are those that appear to concede auspiciousness and inauspiciousness within them, as in *al-Risalah al-Dhahabiyyah*, from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام): "Know that having intercourse with them while the Moon is in the sign of Aries or Aquarius is better, and better than that is for it to be in the sign of Taurus, because it is the exaltation of the Moon."

In *al-Bihar*, from *al-Nawadir*, with its chain to Humran from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): "Whoever travels or marries while the Moon is in Scorpio will not see good." In the book *al-Nujum* by Ibn Tawus, from ‘Ali (عليه السلام): "It is disliked for a man to travel during the wane of the month and when the Moon is in Scorpio."

It is possible to interpret such narrations as being based on dissimulation, as has been said, or as being associated with general augury, as suggested by several narrations commanding charity to repel inauspiciousness. As in the *Nawadir* of al-Rawandi, with its chain to Musa ibn Ja‘far from his father from his grandfather in a hadith: "When you reach the morning, give charity; it will remove from you the inauspiciousness of that day. And when you reach the evening, give charity; it will remove from you the inauspiciousness of that night." It is also possible that this is due to a specific link between the celestial configuration and the earthly event by way of inclination (الاقتضاء).

Among them are those that indicate the denial of the stars' influence on events and a severe prohibition against believing in them or occupying oneself with their science. As in *Nahj al-Balagha*: "The astrologer is like the soothsayer, the soothsayer is like the sorcerer, the sorcerer is like the disbeliever, and the disbeliever is in the Fire." It appears from other reports that confirm them and permit looking into them that the prohibition against occupying oneself with them and building upon them applies only when they are believed to have independent influence, as this leads to polytheism, as mentioned.

Among them are those that indicate it is a truth in itself, except that a little of it does not benefit and much of it cannot be grasped. As in *al-Kafi*, with its chain to ‘Abd al-Rahman ibn Siyabah: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): 'May I be your ransom, people say that looking into the stars is not permissible, yet it pleases me. If it harms my religion, I have no need for anything that harms my religion; but if it does not harm my religion, by Allah, I desire it and I desire to look into it.' He said: 'It is not as they say; it does not harm your religion.' Then he said: 'You look into a great deal of it that cannot be grasped, and a little of it from which no benefit is derived.'"

In *al-Bihar*, from the book *al-Nujum* by Ibn Tawus, from Mu‘awiyah ibn Hakim from Muhammad ibn Ziyad from Muhammad ibn Yahya al-Khath‘ami: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the stars: 'Are they true?' He said to me: 'Yes.' I said to him: 'Is there anyone on earth who knows them?' He said: 'Yes, there is someone on earth who knows them.'" In several narrations: "None knows them except a household from India and a household from the Arabs," and in some: "from Quraysh."

These narrations support what we previously stated: that there is some link between configurations and judgments in a partial sense.

Yes, it is reported in some of these narrations: "Allah sent Jupiter down to earth in the form of a man, and he met a man from the non-Arabs and taught him astrology until he thought he had reached the end. Then he said to him: 'Look, where is Jupiter?' He said: 'I do not see it in the firmament and I do not know where it is.' So he dismissed him and took the hand of a man from India and taught him until he thought he had reached the end, and said: 'Look at Jupiter, where is it?' He said: 'My calculation indicates that you are Jupiter.' He said: 'He gave a gasp and died, and his family inherited his knowledge, so the knowledge is there.'" This report is more akin to a fabrication.

**3 - On Optimism and Augury:** These are the acts of inferring from an event either good and expecting it—which is optimism (التفاؤل)—or evil—which is augury (التطير). They often exert an influence and what is expected from them occurs, whether good or evil, especially in the case of evil; this is a psychological effect.

Islam has distinguished between optimism and augury, commanding optimism and prohibiting augury. In this is a confirmation that the influence within them is a psychological influence.

As for optimism, it is reported from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Be optimistic for good and you shall find it." He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was very optimistic, and this is reported from him in many of his situations.

As for augury, it is reported in several places in the Book from the nations of the prophets during their calls to them, where they would manifest to their prophets that they saw them as an ill omen and thus would not believe. Their prophets answered them with the gist that augury does not turn truth into falsehood nor falsehood into truth, and that the matter belongs to Allah the Glorified, not to the "bird" (the omen) which possesses nothing for itself, let alone possessing good, evil, auspiciousness, or wretchedness for others. The Exalted says: **قَالُوا إِنَّا تَطَيَّرْنَا بِكُمْ لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهُوا لَنَرْجُمَنَّكُمْ وَلَيَمَسَّنَّكُمْ مِنَّا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ قَالُوا طَائِرُكُمْ مَعَكُمْ** — "They said, ‘Indeed we take you for a bad omen. If you do not desist, we will surely stone you, and a painful punishment from us will surely visit you.’ They said, ‘Your bad omen is with yourselves’" (36:18-19); that is, what brings evil upon you is with you, not with us. And He says: **قَالُوا اطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَنْ مَعَكَ قَالَ طَائِرُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ** — "They said, ‘We take you and those with you for a bad omen.’ He said, ‘Your bad omen is with Allah’" (27:47); that is, that which brings you good or evil is with Allah, for He is the One who decrees among you what He decrees, not I and those with me, for we have no part in the matter.

Many reports have been narrated prohibiting augury and repelling its misfortune through disregard, or through trust (التوكل) and supplication. These support what we previously stated: that its influence is among the psychological influences. In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to ‘Amr ibn Hurayth: "Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: 'Augury is according to how you treat it: if you make it light, it becomes light; if you make it severe, it becomes severe; and if you do not make it anything, it will be nothing.'" The indication of the hadith that its influence is psychological is clear. Similar to it is the hadith narrated through Sunni chains: "Three things from which no one is safe: augury, envy, and suspicion." It was asked: "So what should we do?" He said: "When you see an ill omen, proceed; when you feel envy, do not transgress; and when you have suspicion, do not verify it."

In the same vein is what is in *al-Kafi*, from al-Qummi from his father from al-Nawfali from al-Sakkuni from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The expiation for augury is trust (التوكل).'" This is because in trust, one returns the matter of influence to Allah the Exalted, so nothing remains of the thing's effect such that one would be harmed by it. In the same vein is what is reported through Sunni chains, as in the *Nihayah* of Ibn al-Athir: "Augury is polytheism, and there is none of us but [feels it], but Allah removes it through trust."

Regarding the previous meaning, it is reported from Musa ibn Ja‘far (عليه السلام) that he said: "Misfortune for the traveler on his path is in seven things: the crow cawing on his right; the dog with its tail spread; the howling wolf that howls in a man's face while sitting on its haunches, then rising and lowering three times; the deer crossing from right to left; the hooting owl; the gray-haired woman showing her private parts; and the maimed she-ass—that is, one with a cut ear. Whoever feels something in his soul from these should say: 'I seek protection in You, O Lord, from the evil of what I find in my soul,' and he will be protected from that."

To this theological discussion belongs the talk on the inauspiciousness of other matters counted by the common folk as ill-omened and inauspicious, such as sneezing once when intending a matter, and so on. Narrations prohibiting augury regarding them and commanding trust at such times have been reported in various chapters. In the Prophetic report narrated by both groups: "There is no contagion (عدوى), no augury (طيرة), no owl-spirit (هامة), no misfortune (شؤم), no Safar-omen (صفر), no suckling after weaning, no returning to nomadism after migration, no silence for a whole day until night, no divorce before marriage, no manumission before ownership, and no orphanhood after maturity."

* * *

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِالنُّذُرِ** — "Thamud denied the warnings" (54:23). Warnings (النذر) is either a verbal noun, as has been said, meaning: Thamud denied the warning of their prophet Salih (عليه السلام); or it is the plural of 'nadhir' (نذير) in the sense of "the warner," meaning: Thamud denied the prophets, because their denial of one of them is a denial of all of them, as their message is one without difference. Thus, it would carry the same meaning as His saying: **كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ** — "Thamud denied the apostles" (26:141). Or it is the plural of 'nadhir' in the sense of "warning," which returns to one of the two previous meanings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقَالُوا أَبَشَرًا مِنَّا وَاحِدًا نَتَّبِعُهُ إِنَّا إِذًا لَفِي ضَلَالٍ وَسُعُرٍ** — "They said, ‘Shall we follow a lone human from among ourselves? Then we would indeed be in error and madness’" (54:24). This is branched from the denial. Madness (السعر) is the plural of 'sa‘ir' (سعير) meaning a blazing fire. It has also been suggested to mean insanity, which is more suitable to the context. It appears that "lone" (واحدا) refers to numerical oneness. The meaning is: they denied him and said: "Shall we follow a human of our own kind who is a single person, having no retinue or groups with him? If we did, we would indeed be settled in a strange error and insanity."

Thus, this statement would be their justification for not following Salih due to his lack of numbers and power, as they were accustomed to following those who possessed such things, like kings and grandees. Salih (عليه السلام) was calling them to obey him and to reject the obedience of their grandees, as Allah the Glorified recounts from him: **فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ وَلَا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ** — "So be wary of Allah and obey me, and do not obey the dictates of the profligate" (26:151-152).

If "lone" were taken as "one of a kind," the meaning would be: "Shall we follow a human who is one of us—that is, like us and of our kind?" The following verse would then be an explanation of it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَأُلْقِيَ الذِّكْرُ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ بَيْنِنَا بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ** — "Has the Reminder been cast upon him from among us? Rather he is an insolent liar" (54:25). The interrogation, like the previous one, is for denial. The meaning is: "Has the revelation been sent down upon him and he been singled out with it from among us, when he has no merit over us? That can never be." The use of "casting" (الإلقاء) rather than "sending down" or the like is to suggest haste, as has been said.

It is possible that the meaning is to negate that he should be singled out with the casting of the Reminder from among them when he is a human like them. If revelation were true and it were possible for it to descend upon a human, it would have descended upon all humans; so why was he singled out with that which is of a nature to be granted to everyone? Thus, the verse would carry the same meaning as their saying to him: **مَا أَنْتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنَا** — "You are nothing but a human like us" (26:154).

His saying: **بَلْ هُوَ كَذَّابٌ أَشِرٌ** — "Rather he is an insolent liar" means one of extreme arrogance and pride, who wishes to make himself great over us by this means.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَيَعْلَمُونَ غَدًا مَنِ الْكَذَّابُ الْأَشِرُ** — "Tomorrow they will know who is the insolent liar" (54:26). This is a recounting of the speech of the Glorified to Salih (عليه السلام), as are the two verses following it.

What is meant by "tomorrow" (غدا) is the ultimate outcome, from the saying: "Indeed, with today is a tomorrow." The Glorified alludes by it to the punishment that will descend upon them, at which point they will know with the knowledge of direct vision (علم عيان) who the insolent liar is—Salih or themselves?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا مُرْسِلُو النَّاقَةِ فِتْنَةً لَهُمْ فَارْتَقِبْهُمْ وَاصْطَبِرْ** — "We are sending the she-camel as a trial for them; so watch them and be patient" (54:27). This is in the position of providing the reason for what He informed—that the punishment will descend upon them. The sense is: the punishment will descend upon them because We are doing such-and-such. Trial (الفتنة) means examination and testing. The meaning is: We are sending—by way of miracle—the she-camel they requested as a test for them; so wait for them and be patient with their harm.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنَبِّئْهُمْ أَنَّ الْمَاءَ قِسْمَةٌ بَيْنَهُمْ كُلُّ شِرْبٍ مُحْتَضَرٌ** — "And inform them that the water is to be shared between them; each share of water being attended [by the turn-holder]" (54:28). The first plural pronoun refers to the people, and the second to both the people and the she-camel by way of predominance (التغليب). Sharing (القسمة) means that which is divided. Share of water (الشرب) is the portion of drinking water. The meaning is: and inform them after the sending of the she-camel that the water is divided between the people and the she-camel; each portion of the drinking water shall be attended by its owner. Thus, the people attend during their turn and the she-camel during its turn. The Exalted says: **قَالَ هَٰذِهِ نَاقَةٌ لَهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ** — "He said, ‘This is a she-camel: she shall have a [day’s] share of water, and you shall have a [day’s] share of water on a specified day’" (26:155).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَنَادَوْا صَاحِبَهُمْ فَتَعَاطَىٰ فَعَقَرَ** — "Then they called their companion, and he took [the sword] and hamstrung [her]" (54:29). What is meant by "their companion" is the one who hamstrung the she-camel. Taking (التعاطي) means reaching for or grasping. The meaning is: the people called the one who would hamstring the she-camel to do so, and he reached for the act of hamstringing and hamstrung and killed her.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَكَانُوا كَهَشِيمِ الْمُحْتَظِرِ** — "So how were My punishment and My warnings? Indeed We sent against them a single Cry, and they became like the dry sticks of a corral-builder" (54:30-31). The corral-builder (المحتظر) is the owner of a corral (حظيرة), which is like a wall made to keep livestock. The dry sticks (هشيم) of the corral-builder are the dry wood and the like that the owner of the corral gathers for his livestock. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا** — etc. (54:32). Its commentary has already been provided.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ بِالنُّذُرِ** — "The people of Lot denied the warnings" (54:33). Its commentary has been provided in its counterpart.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حَاصِبًا إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ نَجَّيْنَاهُمْ بِسَحَرٍ** — "Indeed We sent a rain of stones upon them, except the family of Lot, whom We delivered at dawn" (54:34). A rain of stones (الحاصب) is a wind that brings stones and pebbles; what is meant is the wind that was sent and pelted them with stones of baked clay (سجيل منضود).

It is stated in *al-Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "The word 'sahar' (سحر), when it is indefinite, refers to one of the dawns. It is said: 'I saw Zayd at a dawn (سحرا) among dawns.' But if you mean the dawn of your current day, you say: 'I came to him at the dawn (بسحر)'—with a 'fatha'—and 'I came to him at dawn (سحر)'—without nunation." The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **نِعْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنَا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي مَنْ شَكَرَ** — "as a blessing from Us. Thus do We reward those who give thanks" (54:35). "Blessing" (نعمة) is an object of purpose (مفعول له) for "We delivered them"; that is, We delivered them so that it might be a blessing from Us with which We specifically favored them because they were thankful to Us, and the reward for thankfulness to Us is salvation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ أَنْذَرَهُمْ بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ** — "He had certainly warned them of Our seizing, but they disputed the warnings" (54:36). The subject pronoun in "he warned them" refers to Lut (عليه السلام). Seizing (البطشة) is a severe taking with punishment. Disputing (التماري) is persistence in argumentation and casting doubt. Warnings (النذر) means the act of warning. The meaning is: I swear, Lut frightened them with Our severe taking, but they argued against his warning and intimidation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ رَاوَدُوهُ عَنْ ضَيْفِهِ فَطَمَسْنَا أَعْيُنَهُمْ فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ** — "They certainly solicited his guests from him. So We blotted out their eyes: ‘Now taste My punishment and My warnings!’" (54:37). Their soliciting him for his guests was their demand that he hand over his guests—who were the angels—to them. Blotting out (طمس) their eyes is their erasure. His saying: **فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي وَنُذُرِ** — "Now taste My punishment and My warnings!" is a shift (التفات) to addressing them for the purpose of intensification and rebuke. Warnings (النذر) is a verbal noun referring to that which the warning relates to, which is the punishment. The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ صَبَّحَهُمْ بُكْرَةً عَذَابٌ مُسْتَقِرٌّ** — "And certainly an unrelenting punishment visited them at dawn" (54:38). It is stated in *al-Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "His saying: 'at dawn' (بكرة) is an adverb of time. If it is definite, such that you mean the dawn of your day, you say: 'I came to him at dawn (بكرة) and in the morning (غدوة)' without declining them. But 'at dawn' here—having nunation—is indefinite." The "unrelenting" (مستقر) nature of the punishment refers to its befalling them and its not failing to reach them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَذُوقُوا عَذَابِي** — to His saying — **مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ** (54:39-40). Its commentary has already been provided.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ جَاءَ آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ النُّذُرُ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا كُلِّهَا فَأَخَذْنَاهُمْ أَخْذَ عَزِيزٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ** — "Certainly the warnings came to the clan of Pharaoh. They denied all Our signs. So We seized them with the seizing of One mighty and omnipotent" (54:41-42). Warnings (النذر) means the act of warning. His saying: **كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا** — "They denied Our signs" is separated without coordination because it is an answer to a presumed question. It is as if when it was said: "Certainly the warnings came to the clan of Pharaoh," it was asked: "So what did they do?" and it was answered with: "They denied Our signs," and from this was branched His saying: "So We seized them with the seizing of One mighty and omnipotent."

## ( Hadith Discourse )

In *Ruh al-Ma‘ani*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ**: "Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: 'Had Allah not made it easy upon the tongues of human beings, no one from creation would have been able to speak the speech of Allah the Exalted.'"

He said: "And al-Daylami narrated a 'marfu‘' report from Anas similar to it." Then he said: "Perhaps the report of Anas, if authentic, is not a commentary on the verse."

**I say:** It is not far-fetched that the intended meaning is the second one we provided in the commentary of the verse.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: **فَفَتَحْنَا أَبْوَابَ السَّمَاءِ بِمَاءٍ مُنْهَمِرٍ**, he said: "A pouring without drops." **وَفَجَّرْنَا الْأَرْضَ عُيُونًا فَالْتَقَى الْمَاءُ**, he said: "The water of the sky and the water of the earth." **عَلَىٰ أَمْرٍ قَدْ قُدِرَ وَحَمَلْنَاهُ**, meaning Nuh, **عَلَىٰ ذَاتِ أَلْواحٍ وَدُسُرٍ**, he said: "The planks are the ship and the 'dusur' are the nails."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَنَادَوْا صَاحِبَهُمْ**, he said: "Qudar, who hamstrung the she-camel." And regarding His saying: **كَهَشِيمِ**, he said: "Dry grass and plants."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu Yazid from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) in a hadith mentioning the story of the people of Lut, he said: "They overwhelmed him—meaning Lut—until they entered the house. Then Jibra’il cried out to him and said: 'O Lut, leave them.' When they entered, Jibra’il gestured with his finger toward them and their eyes vanished, and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَطَمَسْنَا أَعْيُنَهُمْ**."
